{
  "markdown": "# Python Web Toolkit\n\n[![Tests](https://img.shields.io/badge/tests-331%20passing-brightgreen)](https://github.com/firstunicorn/python-web-toolkit/actions)\n[![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/python-3.10%2B-blue)](https://www.python.org/downloads/)\n[![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT-blue.svg)](LICENSE)\n[![Code Style](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)\n[![Validate Dependencies](https://github.com/firstunicorn/python-web-toolkit/actions/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/firstunicorn/python-web-toolkit/actions/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml)\n\nComprehensive Python web development toolkit organized as a monorepo with 17 independent micro-libraries.\n\n \n Extracted from production backend \n\n**From &#96;backend/src/shared/&#96;:** exceptions/ (base_exceptions.py, business_errors.py, validation.py, retry_logic.py), repositories/ (interfaces.py, base.py), primitives/ (datetime_operations.py, string_operations.py, specification patterns), validators/ (postgres escape/sanitize/validate, string_validators.py), schemas/ (common_fields.py, field_mixins.py), api_contracts/ (common_fields.py, common_responses_dto.py), business_validation/input_validators.py, business_sanitization/input_sanitizers.py. **Total:** ~500 LOC production + 1,085 LOC tests (yes, there are a lot of rigorous tests, because it is shared, universal code).\n\n \n\n**Key benefit:** Those extracted ~500 lines in libraries can now be reused across multiple projects, saving ~65 lines per each use for each micro-library; sixty lines don't sound like much, but imagine if you use several of them and do that a few times per project - it can easily go up to 1000 lines of code in just one project. Add on top of that tests and maintenance - you end up saving hours if not days of work. Bonus is flexibility: use and keep only what you really need right now (with near zero dependencies). We will add new cases and stats and extend the list of components in the future.\n\n \n Examples and real cases of estimated LOC saved per app: \n(those was ultra small apps)\n\n**flow_engine:** ~200-250 lines saved (most repositories, CQRS, exceptions)\n\n**token_generator:** ~150-200 lines saved (full CQRS + events + specifications)\n\n**file_management:** ~100-150 lines saved (infrastructure exceptions + repositories)\n\n**flow_engine saved the most LOC** due to its complex multi-repository architecture.\n\n \n\n## What each micro-library solves\n\n \n here = lost event\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAFTER (using library):\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\nfrom python_outbox_core import IOutboxEvent, CloudEventsFormatter\n\nclass UserCreated(IOutboxEvent):\n    event_type: str = \"user.created\"\n    user_id: int\n\n# event saved in same DB transaction — published later by outbox worker\n# zero lost events, even on crash\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n \n\n \n coupling\n        self.activity_log.record(\"user_created\")    # even more\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAFTER (using library):\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\nfrom python_domain_events import BaseDomainEvent, InProcessEventDispatcher\n\nclass UserCreated(BaseDomainEvent):\n    event_type: str = \"user.created\"\n    user_id: int\n\ndispatcher = InProcessEventDispatcher()\ndispatcher.register(UserCreated, SendWelcomeEmailHandler())\ndispatcher.register(UserCreated, InvalidateCacheHandler())\nawait dispatcher.dispatch(UserCreated(user_id=42))\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n \n\n### Summary\n\n| Library | BEFORE (without) | AFTER (with) |\n|---------|-------------------|--------------|\n| **python-input-validation** | Manual regex for emails, hand-rolled sanitizers | &#96;validate_email()&#96;, &#96;sanitize_string()&#96; — tested, reusable |\n| **sqlalchemy-async-repositories** | Raw SQL or repeated CRUD per entity | &#96;AsyncRepository[Entity]&#96; — generic CRUD + filtering |\n| **sqlalchemy-async-session-factory** | Copy-paste async engine + session boilerplate per project | &#96;create_engine()&#96; / &#96;create_session()&#96; — one-liner setup |\n| **python-structlog-config** | Raw &#96;print()&#96; or inconsistent logging setup | &#96;configure_logging(\"dev\")&#96; — JSON in prod, colored in dev |\n| **fastapi-middleware-toolkit** | Manual CORS / error handler / lifespan wiring | &#96;setup_middleware(app)&#96; — one call configures everything |\n| **gridflow-python-mediator** | Direct handler calls, no cross-cutting concerns | &#96;mediator.send(cmd)&#96; with pipeline behaviors (logging, validation) |\n| **python-cqrs-core** | Business logic mixed into route handlers | &#96;Command&#96; / &#96;Query&#96; objects enforce read-write separation |\n| **python-dto-mappers** | Manual &#96;dict → DTO&#96; conversion in every endpoint | &#96;@auto_map&#96; decorator — zero boilerplate mapping |\n| **fastapi-config-patterns** | Scattered &#96;os.getenv()&#96; calls, no validation | Pydantic &#96;Settings&#96; classes with type-safe env loading |\n| **python-app-exceptions** | Bare &#96;raise Exception(\"...\")&#96; scattered everywhere | Typed hierarchy: &#96;NotFoundError&#96;, &#96;ValidationError&#96;, &#96;ConflictError&#96; |\n| **python-infrastructure-exceptions** | Generic &#96;Exception&#96; for every project | Import and use — &#96;slugify()&#96;, &#96;DateRange&#96;, &#96;Specification&#96; |\n| **postgres-data-sanitizers** | Crashes on null chars / surrogates in Postgres writes | Auto-strip before insert — zero silent data corruption |\n| **python-cqrs-dispatcher** | Wiring commands to handlers manually each time | Auto-dispatch: register handler once, dispatcher routes |\n| **pydantic-response-models** | Inconsistent API response shapes across endpoints | &#96;ApiResponse[T]&#96;, &#96;PaginatedResponse[T]&#96; — uniform contract |\n| **python-domain-events** | Ad-hoc event handling, tight coupling to side-effects | &#96;InProcessEventDispatcher&#96; — register/dispatch with tracing |\n| **python-outbox-core** | Lost events on crash / inconsistent event publishing | Transactional outbox — events saved atomically with data |\n\n## Project Structure\n\nThis toolkit is designed to be integrated into your project as a **subfolder**:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nyour-project/\n├── python-web-toolkit/          # This toolkit (clone/copy here)\n│   ├── packages/\n│   │   ├── python-app-exceptions/\n│   │   ├── pydantic-response-models/\n│   │   └── ... Your project config\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Integration options:**\n1. **Git submodule**: &#96;git submodule add python-web-toolkit&#96;\n2. **Direct clone**: &#96;git clone python-web-toolkit&#96;\n3. **Copy**: Download and place in your project\n\nAll installation commands assume &#96;python-web-toolkit&#96; is a **subfolder** in your project root.\n\n**All commands below assume you're running from your project root** (where &#96;python-web-toolkit/&#96; folder is located).\n\n## Architecture\n\nThis toolkit follows **microservices principles at the library level**:\n- Each package is independently installable\n- Packages are modularized as ≤100 lines per file (enforced by development rules)\n- Zero or minimal dependencies per package\n- Clear separation of concerns\n\n### Dependency Architecture\n\n**Primitives Layer** packages are strictly isolated with no cross-dependencies.\n\n**Domain Layer** packages may depend on Primitives, but not on Application layer.\n\n**Application Layer** packages may have lightweight dependencies:\n- Application packages may import from domain and primitives packages\n- CQRS/mediator packages integrate together\n- All packages remain independently deployable\n\nThis layered architecture is enforced via Import Linter (see Architecture Validation section).\n\n## Package Catalog\n\nAll packages are **v0.1.0** and independently installable.\n\n### Core Primitives \u0026 Utilities (Layer: Primitives)\n- **python-technical-primitives** - Text, datetime, and specification pattern utilities\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install python-technical-primitives\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n- **python-app-exceptions** - Application-level exception hierarchy\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install python-app-exceptions\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n- **python-infrastructure-exceptions** - Infrastructure exception types (database, cache, storage)\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install python-infrastructure-exceptions\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n- **python-input-validation** - Email and string validation/sanitization\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install python-input-validation\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n- Structured logging configuration presets (dev/prod/test)\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install python-structlog-config\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### CQRS \u0026 Mediator Pattern (Layer: Domain)\n- **python-cqrs-core** - CQRS interfaces (ICommand, IQuery, BaseCommand, BaseQuery)\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install python-cqrs-core\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n- **gridflow-python-mediator** - Generic mediator with pipeline behaviors\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install gridflow-python-mediator\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n- **python-cqrs-dispatcher** - CQRS dispatcher integrating commands/queries with DTO transformations\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install python-dto-mappers\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n- **pydantic-response-models** - Standard API response DTOs using Pydantic (framework-agnostic)\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install pydantic-response-models\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Database \u0026 Repository Pattern (Layer: Application)\n- **sqlalchemy-async-repositories** - Async repository pattern implementation\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install sqlalchemy-async-repositories\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### FastAPI Extensions (Layer: Application)\n- **fastapi-config-patterns** - Reusable Pydantic settings classes\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install fastapi-config-patterns\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n- **fastapi-middleware-toolkit** - FastAPI middleware setup (CORS, error handlers, lifespan)\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install fastapi-middleware-toolkit\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Event-Driven / Outbox Pattern (Layer: Domain)\n- **python-outbox-core** - Transactional outbox pattern with CloudEvents formatters\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n  pip install python-outbox-core\n  &#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### Option 1: Install Entire Workspace (Recommended for Development)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n# From python-web-toolkit root\npoetry install\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis installs all packages in editable mode with cross-references working automatically.\n\n**Benefits:**\n- ✅ One command installs everything\n- ✅ Cross-package imports work automatically\n- ✅ Shared virtual environment\n- ✅ Consistent dependency resolution\n\n### Option 2: Install Individual Packages\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n# Install specific package\ncd packages/python-cqrs-core\npoetry install\n\n# Run tests\npoetry run pytest -v\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Use when:** Working on a single package in isolation.\n\n**⚠️ Note:** Some packages have cross-dependencies within the monorepo:\n- &#96;python-cqrs-dispatcher&#96; requires &#96;python-cqrs-core&#96; + &#96;gridflow-python-mediator&#96;\n- &#96;sqlalchemy-async-repositories&#96; may require specific Python constraints\n\nFor packages with cross-dependencies, use **Option 1 (Workspace)** instead.\n\n### Option 3: Bulk Install with Script (Recommended for CI/CD)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n# From python-web-toolkit root\n.\\scripts\\install-all.ps1\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Benefits:**\n- ✅ Progress tracking per package\n- ✅ Error handling and reporting\n- ✅ Colored output for quick scanning\n- ✅ CI/CD friendly\n\n### Option 4: Quick One-Liner (For Experienced Developers)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n# From python-web-toolkit root\nGet-ChildItem packages -Directory | ForEach-Object {\n    cd $_.FullName; poetry install --quiet\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Use when:** You need a quick manual install without script overhead.\n\n### Option 5: Individual pip Installs (For Custom Setups)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Install all packages in editable mode with pip\n# Run from your project root (where python-web-toolkit folder is located)\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-app-exceptions\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/pydantic-response-models\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/sqlalchemy-async-repositories\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-technical-primitives\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/postgres-data-sanitizers\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-input-validation\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/fastapi-middleware-toolkit\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/fastapi-config-patterns\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/sqlalchemy-async-session-factory\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-structlog-config\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-infrastructure-exceptions\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-dto-mappers\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-cqrs-core\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/gridflow-python-mediator\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-cqrs-dispatcher\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-outbox-core\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis is **LOCAL installation** from your filesystem, **NOT from PyPI**.\n\n## What &#96;pip install -e&#96; Does:\n\n**&#96;-e&#96;** = **\"editable mode\"** (also called \"development mode\")\n\nWhen you run:\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npip install -e ./python-web-toolkit/packages/python-app-exceptions\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIt means:\n1. ✅ Install the package from **LOCAL filesystem** (the &#96;./python-web-toolkit/packages/...&#96; path)\n2. ✅ Install in **editable mode** - changes to source code are immediately active (no need to reinstall)\n3. ❌ Does **NOT** download from PyPI\n\n## Why Use Editable Mode?\n\nPerfect for **monorepo development**:\n- You edit &#96;python-app-exceptions/src/...&#96; files\n- Changes are instantly available to other packages or projects\n- No need to rebuild/reinstall after every change\n\n## Install from PyPI\n\nIf you need these packages **from PyPI**, you'd install normally:\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npip install python-app-exceptions  # Downloads from pypi.org\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**When to use &#96;-e .&#96;:** You need fine-grained control over which packages to install with pip.\n\n**📝 Note about &#96;-e&#96; flag:**\n- &#96;-e&#96; = **editable/development mode** - installs from **LOCAL filesystem**, NOT from PyPI\n- Source code changes take effect immediately (no reinstall needed)\n- Paths are relative to your project structure\n- Perfect for monorepo development where packages are not yet published or custom modifications required\n\n## Development\n\n### Running All Tests\n\n**Option 1: Workspace (Fastest)**\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n# From python-web-toolkit root\npoetry run pytest\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Option 2: Test Script with Summary (CI/CD)**\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n.\\scripts\\test-all.ps1\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nProvides detailed summary with pass/fail counts per package.\n\n**Option 3: Quick One-Liner (Manual)**\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\nGet-ChildItem packages -Directory | ForEach-Object {\n    cd $_.FullName; poetry run pytest -v --tb=short\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Option 4: Single Package**\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\ncd packages/python-cqrs-core\npoetry run pytest -v\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Option 5: All Packages with Import Mode (Advanced)**\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Test all micro-library packages (from your project root)\npoetry run pytest python-web-toolkit/packages/ --import-mode=importlib -v\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Option 6: Save Test Output to Log**\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n# Save detailed test output (from your project root)\npoetry run pytest python-web-toolkit/packages/ --import-mode=importlib -v 2\u003e\u00261 | Tee-Object -FilePath tests/logs/micro_libs_all.txt\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Code Quality Standards\n\n- **Architecture**: OOP, DRY, SOLID principles, Layered Architecture\n- **Line limit**: 100 lines per file (absolute maximum: 120)\n- **Organization**: Split into sub-modules when approaching limit\n- **Test coverage**: Comprehensive unit + property-based tests\n- **Import rules**: Enforced via Import Linter (see Architecture section)\n\n### Architecture Validation\n\n**Import Linter** enforces architectural boundaries (122 files, 151 dependencies analyzed):\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n# Check import rules\n.\\scripts\\check-architecture.ps1\n# Or directly: poetry run lint-imports\n\n# Run with tests\npoetry run pytest \u0026\u0026 poetry run lint-imports\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**3 Architectural Contracts (All Passing ✓):**\n\n1. **Primitives Layer Cannot Import Domain/Application** - Bottom layer stays isolated\n - &#96;python-technical-primitives&#96;, &#96;python-app-exceptions&#96;, &#96;python-infrastructure-exceptions&#96;\n - ✗ Cannot import: CQRS, mediator, repositories, FastAPI, DTOs\n\n2. **Domain Layer Cannot Import Application** - Mid layer depends only on primitives\n - &#96;python-cqrs-core&#96;, &#96;gridflow-python-mediator&#96;, &#96;pydantic-response-models&#96;, &#96;python-dto-mappers&#96;\n - ✗ Cannot import: &#96;python-cqrs-dispatcher&#96;, repositories, FastAPI middleware\n\n3. **Core Components Independence** - Prevents circular dependencies\n - &#96;python-cqrs-core&#96; and &#96;gridflow-python-mediator&#96; must not import each other\n\n**Layer Hierarchy:**\n\n| Layer | Position | Packages | Import Rules |\n|-------|----------|----------|--------------|\n| **Application** | Top | &#96;python-cqrs-dispatcher&#96; &#96;sqlalchemy-async-repositories&#96; &#96;fastapi-middleware-toolkit&#96; &#96;fastapi-config-patterns&#96; | ✅ Can import from any layer |\n| **Domain** | Middle | &#96;python-cqrs-core&#96; &#96;gridflow-python-mediator&#96; &#96;pydantic-response-models&#96; &#96;python-dto-mappers&#96; &#96;python-input-validation&#96; &#96;python-outbox-core&#96; | ✅ Can import primitives ❌ Cannot import application |\n| **Primitives** | Bottom | &#96;python-technical-primitives&#96; &#96;python-app-exceptions&#96; &#96;python-infrastructure-exceptions&#96; &#96;postgres-data-sanitizers&#96; &#96;sqlalchemy-async-session-factory&#96; &#96;python-structlog-config&#96; | ❌ Cannot import domain/application (Fully isolated foundation) |\n\n**Text Summary:**\n- **Primitives** (bottom): &#96;python-technical-primitives&#96;, exceptions → Cannot import domain/application\n- **Domain** (middle): &#96;python-cqrs-core&#96;, &#96;gridflow-python-mediator&#96;, DTOs → Cannot import application\n- **Application** (top): &#96;python-cqrs-dispatcher&#96;, repositories, FastAPI → Can import anything\n\n### Adding a New Package\n\n1. Create package structure:\n   &#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n   cd packages\n   poetry new my-new-package\n   &#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n2. Add to workspace &#96;pyproject.toml&#96;:\n   &#96;&#96;&#96;toml\n   my-new-package = {path = \"packages/my-new-package\", develop = true}\n   &#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n3. Install workspace:\n   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n   poetry install\n   &#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Documentation\n\n- [Examples Overview](examples/README.md) - All 15 examples organized by use case\n- [Quick Start Guide](examples/QUICK_START.md) - 5 essential examples to get started\n- [Domain Layer Examples](examples/domain/) - Business logic and data patterns\n- [Infrastructure Examples](examples/infrastructure/) - APIs, database, CQRS, messaging\n\n## Publishing to PyPI\n\nPublishing is automated via GitHub Actions using OpenID Connect trusted publishing (no tokens required).\n\n**Setup (one-time):**\n1. Configure trusted publisher at https://test.pypi.org/manage/account/publishing/:\n - PyPI project name: &#96; &#96;\n - Owner: &#96; &#96;\n - Repository name: &#96;python-web-toolkit&#96;\n - Workflow name: &#96;publish-testpypi.yml&#96;\n - Environment name: &#96;testpypi&#96;\n\n2. Repeat for production PyPI at https://pypi.org/manage/account/publishing/ using &#96;publish-pypi.yml&#96; workflow.\n\n**Publishing workflow:**\n1. Bump version in package &#96;pyproject.toml&#96; files (e.g., 0.1.0 → 0.1.1)\n2. Commit and push changes\n3. Run workflow:\n - TestPyPI: Go to Actions → \"Publish to TestPyPI\" → Run workflow\n - Production: Create GitHub release with version tag (e.g., &#96;v0.1.1&#96;)\n\n**Local testing:**\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\n.\\scripts\\build-all.ps1\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## License\n\nMIT\n",

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

No, those gamification events are **not** hardcoded in the Eventing microservice's production code. 

They are used purely as examples. Based on a scan of the codebase, the terms `gamification`, `XPAwarded`, `LevelUpAchieved`, and `StreakUpdated` only appear in two places:

1. **Documentation (`docs/`)**: Used to illustrate how different services (like a gamification-service) would structure their events using the universal `BaseEvent` contract.
2. **Tests (`tests/`)**: Used as dummy payload data in unit and integration tests to verify that the event bus, outbox repository, and consumer idempotency logic work correctly with real-world-looking data. 

The `python-eventing` package itself is completely domain-agnostic. It provides generic infrastructure (like `BaseEvent`, `SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository`, and `EventEnvelopeFormatter`) that any microservice can extend to publish its own specific payloads.

> **Note:** Similarly, these are purely theoretical examples of how other microservices might use the eventing contracts.

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

I've figured out why the API Reference page was showing up empty on Read the Docs!

**The Issue:**
The `docs/source/api/index.md` file was acting as a proxy—it was just a file containing a `# API reference` heading and a `toctree` directive pointing to the dynamically generated `../autoapi/index`. 

While this technically works, the Sphinx Furo theme handles `toctree` directives by hiding them from the page body and moving them into the sidebar. However, because it was nested inside a Markdown file, Furo/MyST on Read the Docs failed to hoist the auto-generated module links up to the global sidebar navigation tree. As a result, you got a page with just a title and no contents!

**The Fix:**
I have deleted the intermediate `docs/source/api/index.md` page entirely, and updated the main `docs/source/index.md` to point its `toctree` directly to `autoapi/index`. This allows the Sphinx AutoAPI extension to natively render its own generated index (which has all the proper submodules and Furo support built-in) right into the navigation structure.

I have created a commit with these changes:
`docs: fix empty API reference page on Read the Docs`

Once this commit is pushed, the next Read the Docs build will properly populate the API Reference your API!

---

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

I found and fixed the root cause! The issue was in the Sphinx AutoAPI configuration:

**The Problem:**
Line 48 in `docs/source/conf.py` had:
```python
autoapi_add_toctree_entry = False
```

This setting tells AutoAPI to generate the documentation files but **not** automatically add itself to the navigation tree. When we removed `api/index.md`, there was nothing left to link the API docs into the sidebar, so they became completely orphaned (404).

**The Fix:**
I've changed it to:
```python
autoapi_add_toctree_entry = True
```

And removed the manual `autoapi/index` entry from the main toctree in `index.md`, since AutoAPI will now add itself automatically.

This means:
- AutoAPI will automatically inject "API Reference" into the navigation sidebar
- It will appear consistently on all pages (event-catalog, integration-guide, etc.)
- The `/api/index.html` redirect will no longer exist (instead, the API docs will live at `/autoapi/index.html`)

I've amended the previous commit with these changes. Push it again and Read the Docs will rebuild with the working navigation!

+## Trusted backend fact examples
 
 &gt; **Note:** Similarly, these are purely theoretical examples of how other microservices might use the eventing contracts.
 
@@ -54,7 +54,7 @@ Facts-only means producers send stable identifiers, timestamps, and transport

-# Integration Guide
+# Integration guide
 
 Use the &#96;python-eventing&#96; package when a domain service needs both local
 in-process dispatch and reliable cross-service publication.
 
-## Install the Package
+## Install the package
 
 Use a normal package dependency instead of a source checkout:
 
@@ -14,7 +14,7 @@ python-eventing = "^0.1.0"
 The published distribution name is &#96;python-eventing&#96;, while Python imports stay
 &#96;from eventing ...&#96;.

+## Add a new event schema
 
 1. Create a Pydantic event model that extends
    &#96;eventing.core.contracts.BaseEvent&#96;.
@@ -26,7 +26,7 @@ business identifiers, timestamps, correlation ids, and metadata only. Do not
 encode downstream reward outcomes such as XP amounts or streak changes in the
 producer payload.

+## Register and dispatch
 
 1. Register the event class in an &#96;EventRegistry&#96;.
 2. Add dispatcher registrations through
@@ -39,7 +39,7 @@ The higher-level &#96;EventBus&#96; also exposes decorator registration, pluggable
 dispatch backends, success/failure/global hooks, and tracing/debug/disable
 hooks while keeping the current outbox and Kafka data plane unchanged.

+## Publish through the outbox
 
 1. Persist the domain event with
    &#96;eventing.infrastructure.outbox.OutboxEventHandler&#96;.
@@ -48,7 +48,7 @@ hooks while keeping the current outbox and Kafka data plane unchanged.
 4. Route terminal failures through
    &#96;eventing.infrastructure.messaging.DeadLetterHandler&#96;.
 
-## Consume Idempotently
+## Consume idempotently
 
 Create inbound consumers by extending
 &#96;eventing.infrastructure.messaging.IdempotentConsumerBase&#96;. This keeps
@@ -60,13 +60,13 @@ example, gamification extensions can map &#96;learning.lesson_completed&#96; into
 &#96;RecordTrustedActivity&#96; without teaching the eventing service about lesson or
 quiz reward logic.
 
-## Optional Later Extension
+## Optional later extension
 
 If the platform later needs replay tooling, DLQ inspection, or event-catalog
 operations, add a small Eventing ops/admin service. Keep it off the hot path:
 services still publish locally through the package, their own outbox, and Kafka.

 ## Trusted Backend Fact Examples

 base fields from &#96;BaseEvent&#96;, but their payloads stay facts-only.

diff --git a/.github/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml b/.github/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml
index 3a5189a..d860da0 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml
+++ b/.github/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml
@@ -16,8 +16,24 @@ jobs:
         with:
           python-version: "3.12"
 
+      - name: Detect Poetry version from lock file
+        id: poetry-version
+        run: |
+          version="$(python - &lt;&lt;'PY'
+          import re
+          from pathlib import Path
+
+          first_line = Path("poetry.lock").read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines()[0]
+          match = re.search(r"Poetry ([0-9]+(?:\.[0-9]+)+)", first_line)
+          if not match:
+              raise SystemExit("Could not determine Poetry version from poetry.lock")
+          print(match.group(1))
+          PY
+          )"
+          echo "version=$version" &gt;&gt; "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
+
       - name: Install Poetry
-        run: pip install poetry==2.3.3
+        run: pip install "poetry==${{ steps.poetry-version.outputs.version }}"
 
       - name: Check pyproject.toml validity
         run: poetry check

commit a805228ecf752d49d841992743e505dc8b751637
Author: FirstUnicorn &lt;fl1xdf8u@anonaddy.me&gt;
Date:   Fri Apr 3 16:00:31 2026 +0300

    ci: add dependency validation workflow

diff --git a/.github/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml b/.github/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..3a5189a
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.github/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml
@@ -0,0 +1,49 @@
+name: Validate Dependencies
+
+on:
+  push:
+    branches: [master, main]
+  pull_request:
+
+jobs:
+  validate:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    steps:
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - name: Set up Python
+        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
+        with:
+          python-version: "3.12"
+
+      - name: Install Poetry
+        run: pip install poetry==2.3.3
+
+      - name: Check pyproject.toml validity
+        run: poetry check
+
+      - name: Verify lock file is up to date
+        run: poetry check --lock
+
+      - name: Verify lock file is properly synced
+        run: |
+          cp poetry.lock poetry.lock.original
+          poetry lock
+
+          if ! diff -q poetry.lock.original poetry.lock; then
+            echo "ERROR: poetry.lock is out of sync with pyproject.toml"
+            echo ""
+            echo "Differences found:"
+            diff poetry.lock.original poetry.lock || true
+            echo ""
+            echo "Please run 'poetry lock' locally and commit the updated lock file."
+            exit 1
+          fi
+
+          echo "Lock file is properly synced"
+
+      - name: Test clean installation
+        run: |
+          rm -rf .venv
+          poetry install --with dev,test,docs --sync
+          poetry show
]633;D;0

For example if a function is imported from a submodule into a package then that function is documented in both the submodule and the package.\n\nNote\n\nThe one exception to this rule is that any object imported into a module is not documented by default.\n\nHowever there are multiple options available for controlling what AutoAPI will document.\n\n### Set &#96;__all__&#96; and &#96;A&#96; would be documented.\n\n# mypackage/ init.py\n\nfrom . import B\n\nall = (\"A\", \"func_a\")\n\nclass A:\n...\n\ndef func_a():\n...\n\ndef func_b():\n...\n\n### Configure [autoapi_options](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Foptions) [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#configure-autoapi-options)\n\nThe [autoapi_options](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Foptions) configuration value gives some high level control over what is documented.\n\nFor example you could remove &#96;private-members&#96; from [autoapi_options](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Foptions) and hide your object definitions in private modules.\n\n# package/ init.py\n\nfrom ._submodule import public_function\n\n# package/_submodule.py\n\ndef public_function():\n\"\"\"This public function will be documented only in &#96;package&#96;.\"\"\"\n...\n\ndef private_function()\n\"\"\"This private function won't be documented.\"\"\"\n...\n\nAs another example, you could remove &#96;undoc-members&#96; from [autoapi_options](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Foptions) and only add docstrings for the modules and other entities that you want to be documented.\n\nSee [autoapi_options](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Foptions) for more information on how to use this option.\n\n### Connect to the [autoapi-skip-member](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#event-autoapi-skip-member) event [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#connect-to-the-autoapi-skip-member-event)\n\nThe [autoapi-skip-member](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#event-autoapi-skip-member) event is emitted whenever a template has to decide whether a member should be included in the documentation.\n\nFor example, to document only packages – in other words, to not document submodules – you could implement an event handler in your conf.py like the following.\n\ndef skip_submodules(app, what, name, obj, skip, options):\nif what == \"module\":\nskip = True\nreturn skip\n\ndef setup(sphinx):\nsphinx.connect(\"autoapi-skip-member\", skip_submodules)\n\n### Customise the API Documentation Templates [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#customise-the-api-documentation-templates)\n\nFinally, you can configure what gets rendered by customising the templates. This is a rather heavy handed approach, so it should only be necessary when the other options do not give you the control the you need. You can learn how to customise the templates in the next section: [How to Customise Layout Through Templates](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#customise-templates).\n\n## How to Customise Layout Through Templates [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#how-to-customise-layout-through-templates)\n\nWarning\n\nTemplates control a lot of behaviour, so customising templates can mean that you lose out on new functionality until you update your customised templates after a new release of AutoAPI.\n\nYou can customise the look of the documentation that AutoAPI generates by changing the Jinja2 templates that it uses. The default templates live in the &#96;autoapi/templates&#96; directory of the AutoAPI package. Simply copy whichever templates you want to customise to a local directory and edit them. To get AutoAPI to use these templates, point the [autoapi_template_dir](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Ftemplate%5Fdir) configuration option to your directory. It can be absolute, or relative to the root of the documentation source directory (ie the directory passed to &#96;sphinx-build&#96;).\n\nautoapi_template_dir = '_autoapi_templates'\n\nYour template directory must to follow the same layout as the default templates. For example, to override the Python class and module templates:\n\n_autoapi_templates\n└── python\n├── class.rst\n└── module.rst\n\n## How to Customise the Index Page [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#how-to-customise-the-index-page)\n\nThe index page that AutoAPI creates is generated using a template. So customising the index page follows the same steps as customising a template. Simply edit the &#96;autoapi/templates/index.rst&#96; template with the same steps as [customising a template](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#customise-templates).\n\n### How to Remove the Index Page [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#how-to-remove-the-index-page)\n\nTo remove the index page altogether, turn off the [autoapi_add_toctree_entry](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Fadd%5Ftoctree%5Fentry) configuration option:\n\nautoapi_add_toctree_entry = False\n\nYou will then need to include the generated documentation in the toctree yourself. For example if you were generating documentation for a package called “example”, you would add the following toctree entry:\n\n.. toctree::\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nautoapi/example/index\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nNote that &#96;autoapi/&#96; is the default location of documentation, as configured by [autoapi_root](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Froot). If you change [autoapi_root](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Froot), then the entry that you need to add would change also.\n\n## How to Configure Where Documentation Appears in the TOC Tree [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#how-to-configure-where-documentation-appears-in-the-toc-tree)\n\nThe [autoapi_root](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Froot) configuration option defines where generated documentation is output. To change where documentation is output, simply change this option to another directory relative to the documentation source directory:\n\nautoapi_root = 'technical/api'\n\n## How to Transition to Autodoc-Style Documentation [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#how-to-transition-to-autodoc-style-documentation)\n\nOnce you have written some documentation with the [Autodoc-Style Directives](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/directives.html#autodoc-directives), turning the automatic documentation generation off is as easy as disabling the [autoapi_generate_api_docs](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Fgenerate%5Fapi%5Fdocs) configuration option:\n\nautoapi_generate_api_docs = False\n\n## How to Transition to Manual Documentation [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#how-to-transition-to-manual-documentation)\n\nTo start writing API documentation yourself, you can get AutoAPI to keep its generated files around as a base to start from using the [autoapi_keep_files](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config.html#confval-autoapi%5Fkeep%5Ffiles) option:\n\nautoapi_keep_files = True\n\nOnce you have built your documentation with this option turned on, you can disable AutoAPI altogether from your project.\n\n## How to Include Type Annotations as Types in Rendered Docstrings [¶](https://sphinx-autoapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/how_to.html#how-to-include-type-annotations-as-types-in-rendered-docstrings)\n\nWarning\n\nThis feature is experimental and may change or be removed in future versions.\n\nSince v3.0, &#96;sphinx&#96; has included an &#96;sphinx.ext.autodoc.typehints&#96; extension that is capable of rendering type annotations as parameter types and return types.\n\nFor example the following function:\n\ndef _func(a: int, b: Optional[str]) -\u003e bool\n\"\"\"My function.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n:param a: The first arg.\n:param b: The second arg.\n\n:returns: Something.\n\"\"\"\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nwould be rendered as:\n\n_func(a, b)\n\nParameters:\n\n- a ([int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int)) – The first arg.\n- b (Optional [[str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)]) – The second arg.\n\nReturns:\n\nSomething.\n\nReturn type:\n\n[bool](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#bool)\n\nAutoAPI is capable of the same thing. To enable this behaviour, load the &#96;sphinx.ext.autodoc.typehints&#96;(or [sphinx.ext.autodoc](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/extensions/autodoc.html#module-sphinx.ext.autodoc)) normal:\n\nextensions = ['sphinx.ext.autodoc', 'autoapi.extension']\nautodoc_typehints = 'description'\n\nNote\n\nUnless _**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

Based on the official Sphinx AutoAPI documentation, here are your **options** and **best practices** for handling empty module pages:

## Options for Handling Empty Modules

### 1. **Use `autoapi-skip-member` Event (Recommended)**
Skip empty modules programmatically by connecting to the event in `conf.py`:

```python
def skip_empty_modules(app, what, name, obj, skip, options):
    # Skip modules that only contain a docstring (no classes, functions, etc.)
    if what == "module":
        # Check if module has any actual content
        if not obj.children or len(obj.children) == 0:
            skip = True
    return skip

def setup(sphinx):
    sphinx.connect("autoapi-skip-member", skip_empty_modules)
```

**Pros:**
- Clean, programmatic control
- No template changes needed
- Easy to maintain

### 2. **Define `__all__` in Empty Modules**
Set `__all__ = []` in modules you want to hide:

```python
# src/eventing/application/__init__.py
"""Application layer for the eventing service."""
__all__ = []  # This will hide the module from AutoAPI
```

**Pros:**
- Python-native approach
- Explicit control per module

**Cons:**
- Requires editing every empty module

### 3. **Remove `undoc-members` from `autoapi_options`**
Only document modules with docstrings for their members:

```python
autoapi_options = [
    "members",
    "show-inheritance",
    "show-module-summary",
]
```

**Cons:**
- Too aggressive - hides useful modules too

### 4. **Leave As-Is (Industry Standard)**
Many projects keep empty namespace modules documented with just their docstring.

**Pros:**
- Shows package structure clearly
- Follows Python package conventions
- Users understand the architecture

## Best Practice Recommendation

**Leave the empty modules as they are.** Here's why:

1. **It's standard practice** - Major Python projects (Django, Flask, SQLAlchemy) all show empty namespace packages in their docs
2. **Shows architecture** - Users can see your package structure (`application`, `domain`, `infrastructure`)
3. **Future-proof** - When you add code to these modules later, the docs automatically update
4. **Not actually empty** - They have meaningful docstrings explaining their purpose

## If You Really Want to Hide Them

If the empty pages bother you, use **Option 1** (the `autoapi-skip-member` event). It's the cleanest solution. Would you like me to implement this for you?

### Option 2: Architectural Purpose (Rating: 85/100)

"""Application layer coordination and orchestration.

The application layer acts as a mediator between the domain layer
(business logic) and infrastructure layer (technical implementations).
Currently, this package is a namespace placeholder. Application services
will be added here when cross-cutting orchestration logic is needed
beyond what the domain provides.

For current functionality, see:
- eventing.core.contracts - Domain event definitions
- eventing.infrastructure - Outbox, messaging, persistence implementations
"""

**Cons:** Might be too detailed for an empty module

### Option 3: DDD/Clean Architecture Explanation (Rating: 90/100)
```python
"""Application layer for the eventing service.

Purpose
-------
In Domain-Driven Design architecture, the application layer:
- Orchestrates use cases that span multiple domain objects
- Coordinates transactions across infrastructure boundaries
- Handles application-specific business rules

Current State
-------------
This namespace is reserved for future application services. Current
eventing functionality lives in:

- ``eventing.core.contracts`` - Event schemas and registry
- ``eventing.infrastructure.outbox`` - Transactional outbox pattern
- ``eventing.infrastructure.messaging`` - Kafka integration

When to Add Code Here
---------------------
Add application services when you need to:
- Coordinate multiple infrastructure components in a single use case
- Implement cross-cutting application concerns
- Handle application-level transaction boundaries
"""
```
**Pros:** Very educational, explains when code belongs here  
**Cons:** Long for an empty module, might become outdated

---

### Option 4: Minimal with Link (Rating: 80/100) ⭐ **RECOMMENDED**
```python
"""Application layer namespace for the eventing service.

This layer coordinates use cases and orchestrates infrastructure components.
Currently empty - core functionality is in ``eventing.infrastructure``.

See Also
--------
- eventing.core.contracts : Event definitions and contracts
- eventing.infrastructure : Outbox, messaging, and persistence
"""
```
**Pros:** Concise, informative, uses Sphinx directives properly  
**Cons:** Less educational than Option 3

---

### Option 5: Future-Focused (Rating: 70/100)
```python
"""Application layer (reserved for future use).

This namespace will contain application services that orchestrate
domain logic and infrastructure. See eventing.infrastructure for
current implementations.
"""
```
**Pros:** Very short, forward-looking  
**Cons:** Doesn't explain architectural purpose well

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_

Perfect! I've enhanced all 10 module docstrings across the codebase. Here's what each now includes:

## Enhanced Modules

### 1. **Root Package** (`eventing/__init__.py`)
- Quick start code example
- Overview of key components (core vs infrastructure)
- Shows basic usage pattern

### 2. **Core Layer** (`eventing/core/__init__.py`)
- Explains domain-agnostic event infrastructure
- Lists key classes (BaseEvent, EventBus, EventRegistry)
- Includes working code example with decorator syntax

### 3. **Core Contracts** (`eventing/core/contracts/__init__.py`)
- Organizes exports into categories (Event Definitions, Dispatch Patterns, Envelope Formatting)
- Cross-references to related packages

### 4. **Event Bus Facade** (`eventing/core/contracts/bus/__init__.py`)
- Explains dispatch backend abstractions
- Documents EventBus facade and SequentialDispatchBackend
- Links to hooks and mediator integration

### 5. **Infrastructure Layer** (`eventing/infrastructure/__init__.py`)
- Comprehensive overview of all technical implementations
- Organized by concern (Outbox, Kafka, Persistence, Health)
- Lists all key classes with descriptions

### 6. **Persistence** (`eventing/infrastructure/persistence/__init__.py`)
- Explains outbox pattern persistence
- Documents idempotency tracking
- Clarifies ORM models vs stores

### 7. **Messaging** (`eventing/infrastructure/messaging/__init__.py`)
- Details Kafka publishing and consuming
- Explains idempotency and DLQ patterns
- Links to related stores

### 8. **Health Checks** (`eventing/infrastructure/health/__init__.py`)
- Explains outbox staleness monitoring
- Lists what health checks detect
- Cross-references outbox and presentation

### 9. **Outbox** (`eventing/infrastructure/outbox/__init__.py`)
- **Most detailed** - explains entire transactional outbox pattern
- Documents guarantees (at-least-once, ordering, crash safety)
- Includes 5-step usage pattern

### 10. **Presentation** (`eventing/presentation/__init__.py`)
- Clarifies it's empty namespace
- Points to actual router implementation

## Impact on Documentation

When Read the Docs rebuilds, every module page will now:
- ✅ Explain **why** the module exists
- ✅ List **what's** inside with descriptions
- ✅ Show **how** to use it (where appropriate)
- ✅ Link to **related** modules

No more confusing empty pages - even namespace modules now serve as architectural guides!

Want to push these changes?

    docs: convert headings to sentence case

 &#96;eventing.core.contracts.EventEnvelopeFormatter&#96;, which wraps each event as a

+## Trusted backend fact examples
 
 &gt; **Note:** Similarly, these are purely theoretical examples of how other microservices might use the eventing contracts.
 
@@ -54,7 +54,7 @@ Facts-only means producers send stable identifiers, timestamps, and transport

 effects are computed by the consuming domain service.

@@ -1,9 +1,9 @@
-# Integration Guide
+# Integration guide
 
 Use the &#96;python-eventing&#96; package when a domain service needs both local
 in-process dispatch and reliable cross-service publication.
 
-## Install the Package
+## Install the package
 
 Use a normal package dependency instead of a source checkout:
 
@@ -14,7 +14,7 @@ python-eventing = "^0.1.0"
 The published distribution name is &#96;python-eventing&#96;, while Python imports stay
 &#96;from eventing ...&#96;.
 
-## Add a New Event Schema
+## Add a new event schema
 
 1. Create a Pydantic event model that extends
    &#96;eventing.core.contracts.BaseEvent&#96;.
@@ -26,7 +26,7 @@ business identifiers, timestamps, correlation ids, and metadata only. Do not
 encode downstream reward outcomes such as XP amounts or streak changes in the
 producer payload.
 
-## Register and Dispatch
+## Register and dispatch
 
 1. Register the event class in an &#96;EventRegistry&#96;.
 2. Add dispatcher registrations through
@@ -39,7 +39,7 @@ The higher-level &#96;EventBus&#96; also exposes decorator registration, pluggable
 dispatch backends, success/failure/global hooks, and tracing/debug/disable
 hooks while keeping the current outbox and Kafka data plane unchanged.
 
-## Publish Through the Outbox
+## Publish through the outbox
 
 1. Persist the domain event with
    &#96;eventing.infrastructure.outbox.OutboxEventHandler&#96;.
@@ -48,7 +48,7 @@ hooks while keeping the current outbox and Kafka data plane unchanged.
 4. Route terminal failures through
